design-audit.message: Design audit not yet performed — run /rq:design-audit for UI audit

qa.message: QA testing not yet performed — run /rq:qa for browser testing

findings_details.title: destroy redirects to public tourdates route instead of phoenix admin
findings_details.body: Phoenix::TourdatesController#destroy calls redirect_to(tourdates_url) at line 80. tourdates_url resolves to the public /tourdates route (TourdatesController), not the Phoenix admin index. The branch fixes create/update to use [:phoenix, @tourdate] but missed the same shape in destroy. The correct call is phoenix_tourdates_url, consistent with Phoenix::ArticlesController#destroy which uses phoenix_articles_url.

findings_details.title: let(:freeze_time) is shadowed by a local variable inside the around block and is never used
findings_details.body: Line 7 declares let(:freeze_time) { Time.now }. Line 14 inside the around block reassigns freeze_time as a local variable (freeze_time = Time.now), which shadows the let completely. The let is never referenced — only the local variable is passed to Timecop.freeze. The let(:freeze_time) declaration is dead and should be removed to avoid misleading readers who expect let-defined state to be the source of truth.

findings_details.title: update failure test asserts @tourdate.artist_id equals itself — always true, proves nothing
findings_details.body: Line 167: expect(@tourdate.artist_id).to eq(@tourdate.artist_id). This comparison evaluates both sides from the same object — it is always true regardless of whether the update actually failed or changed the attribute. This test cannot catch a regression where the controller silently accepts an invalid artist_id and commits the change. Fix: capture the original artist_id before the request and assert against that: original_id = @tourdate.artist_id; ... ; @tourdate.reload; expect(@tourdate.artist_id).to eq(original_id)

findings_details.title: GET #index test combines two distinct assertions in one it block
findings_details.body: The it "assigns @articles and @products" block at line 9 makes two distinct assertions: expect(assigns(:articles)) and expect(assigns(:products)). These test two independent controller responsibilities. A failure provides ambiguous signal — it is unclear which assignment failed. Per project conventions (all other specs in this file use one assertion per it), split into two separate examples: it "assigns @articles" and it "assigns @products".

findings_details.title: Mass-assignment via params[:tourdate] in create and update — no permit list
findings_details.body: TourdatesController#create (line 55) passes params[:tourdate] directly to Tourdate.new without calling .permit(). Same pattern in #update (line 68). Any parameter key the attacker submits is accepted, including internal fields like id or timestamps. Tracked in https://github.com/jerrod/lovitt-modern/issues/53 and explicitly noted as out of scope for this PR. Flagging at info level per review instructions.

findings_details.title: destroy redirects to public tourdates_url instead of phoenix_tourdates_url
findings_details.body: TourdatesController#destroy (line 80) calls redirect_to(tourdates_url) which routes to the public-facing tourdates page, not the Phoenix admin index. After deleting a tourdate the admin is silently redirected out of the admin panel. This PR fixed create/update redirects but left destroy unaddressed. Tracked in https://github.com/jerrod/lovitt-modern/issues/67. The test at spec line 190 asserts redirect_to(tourdates_url) — it passes but asserts the wrong URL. Fix: redirect_to phoenix_tourdates_url and update the test assertion.

findings_details.title: Destroy spec asserts the wrong redirect URL (public tourdates_url)
findings_details.body: Line 190 asserts redirect_to(tourdates_url), which matches the current broken production behaviour (admin is bounced to the public page). The test is testing the bug, not the desired behaviour. When issue #67 is fixed, this test must be updated to expect phoenix_tourdates_url. Flagging now so the test is not treated as a green confirmation of correct behaviour.

findings_details.title: Test seeds Lovitt Records artist to mask a model-layer nil crash, not to test a feature
findings_details.body: Article#artists (app/models/article.rb:41) does Artist.where(name: 'Lovitt Records').first as a fallback when no artist matches the article content. If that artist row does not exist in the database, the method returns [nil] rather than an empty array, and the view iterates over it and crashes. The spec at line 23 seeds create(:artist, name: 'Lovitt Records') to prevent the crash, but the underlying bug — the model returns nil inside the links array — is unfixed. The correct fix is in the model: change line 41 to 'links << Artist.where(name: "Lovitt Records").first' followed by a compact call (links.compact.uniq.reverse), or guard with '&.' so a missing Lovitt Records row yields an empty array rather than [nil]. Until the model is fixed, any environment missing that artist seed (production data loss, fresh staging DB, future test that forgets the seed) will 500 the public show page.

findings_details.title: render ... and return false idiom — should be render ... and return (omit false)
findings_details.body: Line 13 uses 'render status: 404, file: "..." and return false unless @article'. The boolean false as the return value of the action method is non-idiomatic; the conventional form is 'render(...) and return'. Functionally equivalent here because Rails ignores the controller action's return value, but 'return false' is a Rails 2-era pattern that pre-dates ActionController::Metal and reads as if it signals something (it does not). Consistent with ProductsController#show:18 which has the same idiom — both should be updated to 'and return'. Low severity because no bug today, but it misleads readers about intent.
